books.title: Leading Complex Projects
books.author: IDEO U
books.description:
I already knew how to manage a production. Call sheets, spreadsheets, run of shows. This course taught me something different: how to lead one. It draws from design, psychology, and leadership to focus on the human side of complex projects.

It gave me new insight into how to build alignment across a team, how to balance logic and intuition when plans change, and how to understand what motivates the people you're working with. I left with a deeper philosophy for how to navigate complexity, building on the hard skills I'd already built throughout my career.

books.title: Watch Me Play
books.author: T.L. Taylor
books.description:
Throughout my career I'd noticed that streamers on Twitch often draw more viewers and audience participation than most big budget corporate productions. This book helped me understand why.

The internet was designed for people to navigate, explore, and make choices about what to do next. Taylor studied Twitch as the platform where live streaming emerged from that same principle, as a social experience where the audience and the streamer move through the content together.

Most traditional events and productions treat a live stream as a way to broadcast a pre-determined show to a passive audience. As Gen Z and Gen Alpha enter adulthood, they bring entirely different expectations around interactivity and participation when it comes to live streaming, and that gap is only going to widen.

This book taught me to treat every live stream as a conversation with an audience, and over time, a dialogue with a community.

books.title: AI for Designers
books.author: Interaction Design Foundation
books.description:
I was already using large language models before taking this course, but in a very experimental way. No methods, no training, just trial and error.

This course showed me how leading design professionals were approaching these tools with real intentionality, treating the way you interact with a model as its own design problem. What context you share, what frameworks you bring in, how you frame what you're looking for, all of it influences the kind of outcome you get and how useful it actually is.

books.title: The Art of Gathering
books.author: Priya Parker
books.description:
When someone decides to do a livestream or an event, the conversation almost always starts with logistics: what platform will we use, what gear do we need, how many cameras? Parker makes a distinction that stayed with me: most gathering advice focuses on preparing the thing rather than preparing the people. Her core argument is that a category is not a purpose. "Livestream" is a category, but it doesn't answer the question of why you're gathering people in the first place.

Many of the projects I've worked on had no answer to that question. Someone planned an event, decided to livestream it, and the entire production was built around getting the technology to work. Nobody asked what the experience was supposed to do for the people showing up. Parker showed me that gathering people is a design discipline, and that the technology is there to support that intention, not replace it.

books.title: Good Strategy Bad Strategy
books.author: Richard Rumelt
books.description:
Most organizations have something they call a strategy: grow the audience, increase engagement, build brand awareness. Rumelt's argument is that no matter how warm and fuzzy your list of goals and aspirations makes you feel, it doesn't mean you have a strategy. You can't have a strategy until you first diagnose the challenge that stands in your way.

Once you can name what's actually difficult, you build what he calls a guiding policy: the overarching approach you'll commit to for addressing that challenge. Then you design coherent actions, the specific steps that carry out that policy. Without the original diagnosis, you're just throwing things out and hoping they stick. The goals might sound ambitious, but there's no mechanism for getting there. He goes deeper into this idea in his follow-up book, *The Crux*.

books.title: Thinking in Systems
books.author: Donella Meadows
books.description:
The word "system" gets used constantly in production and business, but most people couldn't explain how a system actually works. Meadows wrote one of the clearest explanations I've found. She describes a system as a set of connected parts organized to produce a specific outcome, and shows how the behavior that emerges over time reveals the structure behind it. Recognizing those patterns is the key to understanding why projects perform well or why they break down.

One concept that stuck with me is what she calls the drift to low performance, the idea that standards don't collapse overnight. Each small decline resets what people on the team perceive as acceptable, and over time the whole system builds on itself in the wrong direction until nobody can point to the moment things changed. A recurring problem almost always traces back to how the work was set up in the first place. It's a good reminder to take a look at the bigger structure, instead of only fixating on the part that broke.

books.title: The Brand Gap
books.author: Marty Neumeier
books.description:
Neumeier's most well-known idea is that a brand is not what you say it is, it's what they say it is. Most people think a brand is how a company presents itself to the world, but it's actually the feeling people carry with them after engaging with the company. You can be intentional about the experiences you create, but you can't dictate what someone walks away thinking and feeling. That belongs to them.

This changed how I think about what live streaming can do for a brand. When people show up, participate, and really connect, they leave with a deeper sense of what the company is all about, and that translates into the way they describe you to other people.

books.title: Building Brand Communities
books.author: Charles Vogl
books.description:
When marketing teams or event planners put together a live stream or event, they almost always talk about building community. Vogl and Jones describe an authentic community as one built on mutual concern for one another, common values, and meaningful experiences together. They contrast this with what they call a mirage community: a group that uses the language of community without any of those conditions.

Once I had that distinction, I could see why so many projects I'd worked on felt hollow when they talked about community. But the book also gave me something to work toward. Through case studies of organizations like Twitch, Salesforce, and Harley-Davidson, it lays out what authentic brand communities actually look like when they're working, and what it takes to build one.

books.title: The Opposable Mind
books.author: Roger Martin
books.description:
When the plan falls apart on site and you need to build a new solution on the fly, how do you decide what to do?

Martin's argument is that the best problem solvers don't choose between the options in front of them. They look for patterns across the available choices and find a way to integrate the best parts of each into something new.

I often turn to this model when solving unexpected challenges on a production. When the original plan breaks, my first move is to look at what's still working and build forward from there, rather than forcing a choice between the obvious options.

He goes deeper into the methodology in his follow-up, *Creating Great Choices*.

books.title: Herzog on Herzog
books.author: Werner Herzog
books.description:
This book is a series of interviews with German filmmaker Werner Herzog, and it completely changed how I think about leading ambitious creative projects.

In his film *Fitzcarraldo*, Herzog was shooting in the Peruvian jungle when his lead actor got sick halfway through and had to leave the production. He recast the role and started filming the entire project over from the beginning. The film's central sequence shows a 320-ton steamship being hauled over a mountain, and Herzog was so committed to capturing it practically that the footage is of real people doing it without special effects.

Herzog's refusal to compromise on his creative vision, no matter what got in his way, continues to inspire me whenever a setback threatens the integrity of a project.

books.title: The Art of Explanation
books.author: Ros Atkins
books.description:
When going live, most large organizations default to pre-approved scripts in a teleprompter. The upside is that the message gets delivered exactly as planned. The downside is that reading from a teleprompter rarely feels organic, spontaneous, or human, and audiences notice.

On the other end of the spectrum, young creators on platforms like Twitch rely on live streaming to build a personal brand. Their spontaneity, edge, and sometimes controversy is what makes them relatable and exciting to watch. Taken to an extreme, it also becomes reckless, and it only works because the cost of getting it wrong is lower for them than it would be for a major corporation.

The best live productions I've worked on land somewhere in between, and finding the balance between preparation and spontaneity is one of the most challenging parts of my work.

Atkins has built a career explaining complex stories in real time on the BBC, and his book is about how to prepare for a live appearance so that your ideas connect with an audience. He offers a method he calls strands and threads, a way to distill complex information into structures you can easily study and recall, so you're prepared to adapt when the conversation shifts, hold your footing when you're questioned, and keep making sense when things don't go the way you planned.

books.title: The Creative Act: A Way of Being
books.author: Rick Rubin
books.description:
This book explores the parts of the creative process that are hardest to explain: where ideas actually come from, why they come to you when you stop trying, and why being receptive to the unexpected is more important than hard work.

In live production, so much of the work is about preparation and control. But the moments that make a show come alive are rarely the ones you scripted. They come from staying present and responsive to what's actually happening in the room. Rubin helped me define a philosophy for my creative work: the most interesting ideas emerge when you're not trying to control everything.

books.title: Design Thinking for Business Growth
books.author: Michael Lewrick
books.description:
In live stream production, most of the creative energy goes into bringing the crew together to execute a complex engineering plan. But you rarely hear about how that plan fits into a bigger picture. Why would we invest money into this? How does it create value? What's the business model?

This book gave me a framework for thinking through these bigger questions. Lewrick uses design thinking to map business ecosystems, tracing how value flows between the people who orchestrate an experience, the people who participate in it, and the organizations that support it. For a live production, that's the production team, the audience, and the brands behind it.

The whole Design Thinking series is worth reading, including *The Design Thinking Playbook*, *The Design Thinking Toolbox*, and *Design Thinking and Innovation Metrics*.

books.title: Value Proposition Design
books.author: Alexander Osterwalder
books.description:
Artists tend to start with what excites them most about their project, and business leaders tend to start with the outcomes they're trying to hit: revenue, growth, and engagement numbers. Both can lose sight of the people they're designing for.

Osterwalder gives you a framework for starting from a different direction: who are you making this for, what do they want to accomplish, and what is getting in their way. Once you've answered those questions, you design something that addresses all three in a meaningful way.

Before I read this book, I knew empathy was important and I knew it was related to value, but I'd never thought about value as something you could design. Osterwalder showed me a method for it that I now use on every project.

I highly recommend Osterwalder's other books in the Strategyzer series: *Business Model Generation*, *Testing Business Ideas*, and *High Impact Tools for Teams*.

books.title: Building A StoryBrand
books.author: Donald Miller
books.description:
Miller argues that most companies make the same marketing mistake: the story they tell is all about them. All their content focuses on what they've built, what they believe, and what makes them special.

The problem is this has nothing to do with what the customer cares about. The brand's story leaves them out, so they stop paying attention.

Miller's reframe: the brand's job is to be the guide. The customer is the hero of their own story. You're Yoda, and your customer is Luke Skywalker.

After reading this book, I realized that many of the shows I've worked on have made the same mistake. The organization is so focused on their own messaging, the exact wording, the exact presentation, that the audience becomes an afterthought.

The demands of a live production can distract from asking important questions like: Why would anyone watching actually care about this? What would make them feel like this experience was made for them? Miller gave me those questions, and they've changed the way I approach every project since. It sounds simple, but it's surprisingly easy to get wrong.

books.title: The Design of Everyday Things
books.author: Don Norman
books.description:
Norman's core idea is that when something feels intuitive to use, that's not by accident. A designer was intentional about how what they're building communicates what it does and how you can interact with it. He illustrates this through the everyday objects we take for granted: a door handle that you intuitively know to turn and pull, a cup handle that tells you where to hold it. He calls this quality an affordance.

That concept shaped how I think about everything I put in front of a crew. How should a run of show be structured so someone can read it under pressure? How should gear be labeled and packed so someone unfamiliar with the setup can find what they need? On a live production, people are constantly picking up documents, systems, and tools with no time to ask questions. If those things aren't designed to be immediately understood, you add unnecessary friction and the work may suffer.
